evals.prompt: We're launching a cold email campaign for FlowOps (workflow automation platform for mid-market logistics companies, 200-2000 employees). Target persona: VP Operations at 3PL companies who are struggling with manual warehouse processes and losing 15-20 hours/week on shipment tracking. The offer is a free warehouse workflow audit. Brand voice: direct, confident, no hype -- sounds like a senior operator talking to a peer. Write email subject line variants for A/B testing. We need to test what angle drives the highest open rate.
evals.expected_output: 8+ subject line variants across at least 4 frameworks (Specificity, Curiosity, Relevance, Urgency, Question), each under 50 characters, with character counts displayed, a testing recommendation section naming the lead A/B pair, expected winner with reasoning, metric (open rate), and minimum sample size.
expectations: Campaign ROI: At least 8 subject line variants are produced, spanning at least 4 of the 5 copywriting frameworks (Specificity, Curiosity, Relevance, Urgency, Question) with each variant labelled by framework -- testing within fewer than 4 frameworks limits the learning surface and means the campaign only discovers local optima within a narrow angle, not which fundamental approach resonates with VP Operations personas

Campaign ROI: Every subject line variant is 50 characters or fewer with character count displayed alongside each -- subject lines that exceed mobile display limits get truncated mid-word, destroying the hook and wasting the send; this is a hard platform constraint, not a style preference

Content performance: Variants test genuinely different angles across the test dimensions (pain vs. gain, specific vs. broad, question vs. statement, rational vs. emotional) -- not synonym swaps of the same message (e.g., 'save time on tracking' vs. 'reduce tracking hours' vs. 'cut tracking time' are the same angle three times and produce no A/B learning)

Pipeline contribution: Subject lines reference the specific persona world of VP Operations at 3PL companies (warehouse processes, shipment tracking, the 15-20 hours/week pain point) rather than generic productivity language -- ICP-specific subject lines outperform generic ones because the recipient recognizes their own situation in the preview pane

Brand consistency: No variant contains banned jargon (leverage, synergy, best-in-class, seamless, robust, solution) and the tone reads like a senior operator talking to a peer -- not like a marketing email, not like a sales pitch, not like a press release

Campaign ROI: A TESTING RECOMMENDATION section is present that names the specific lead A/B test pair, states an expected winner with reasoning tied to the persona's psychology (not just 'shorter subject lines perform better'), specifies open rate as the metric, and includes a minimum sample size -- without this section, the marketing team has 8 variants and no testing strategy, which means they either test randomly or default to gut feel

evals.prompt: I need LinkedIn Sponsored Content ad variants for DataMesh (a real-time data integration platform for enterprise RevOps teams). We're promoting a gated report: '2026 Revenue Operations Benchmark Report -- 847 companies surveyed.' Target persona: Director of Revenue Operations at B2B SaaS companies ($20M-$200M ARR) who are dealing with fragmented data across Salesforce, HubSpot, and Snowflake. Give me at least 3 ad variants -- headline, body, and CTA for each. Also include one Thought Leader ad variant written from the perspective of our CEO. Brand voice: analytical, evidence-first, slightly contrarian -- we challenge conventional RevOps thinking.
evals.expected_output: 3+ LinkedIn Sponsored Content ad variants each with headline (max 150 chars), body (max 600 chars), and CTA button text, plus 1 Thought Leader ad variant (300-600 chars, first-person, conversational), character counts on all elements, and a testing recommendation with expected winner, reasoning, and CTR as the metric.
expectations: Campaign ROI: At least 3 Sponsored Content ad variants are produced, each testing a meaningfully different angle (e.g., one leads with the pain of fragmented data across Salesforce/HubSpot/Snowflake, another leads with a specific benchmark finding, another leads with a contrarian claim that challenges conventional RevOps thinking) -- variants that restate the same message with different words produce no A/B learning and waste ad spend on creative rotation that doesn't inform strategy

Campaign ROI: All headlines are 150 characters or fewer, all bodies are 600 characters or fewer, and the Thought Leader ad is 300-600 characters, with character counts displayed on every element -- LinkedIn silently truncates copy that exceeds platform limits, so the carefully crafted hook gets cut off mid-sentence and the ad spend is wasted on a broken message

Content performance: Every CTA button uses specific action text that describes what the person gets (e.g., 'Download the 2026 Benchmark', 'Get the RevOps Report', 'See How You Compare') -- not generic text like 'Learn More', 'Click Here', or 'Submit' that gives no reason to click and produces lower CTR because the value exchange is unclear

Pipeline contribution: The ad copy targets Directors of Revenue Operations at $20M-$200M ARR B2B SaaS companies specifically -- references their world (Salesforce + HubSpot + Snowflake fragmentation, RevOps team dynamics, benchmark comparison against 847 companies) so the ICP self-selects and non-ICP clicks (wasted spend) are minimized

Content performance: The Thought Leader ad variant reads like a genuine CEO LinkedIn post (first-person, conversational, opinion-driven) and not like a reformatted Sponsored Content ad -- Thought Leader ads that sound like ads get scrolled past because they violate the format's implicit contract with the reader, which is personal perspective from a named human

Campaign ROI: A TESTING RECOMMENDATION section names the specific lead A/B test pair from the variants, states an expected winner with reasoning tied to what Director of RevOps personas respond to (not generic ad theory), and specifies CTR as the primary metric -- this turns the creative deliverable into an executable test plan that the paid media team can launch without additional strategy work

evals.prompt: We're rebuilding the homepage for Clarion (an AI-powered sales coaching platform that analyses recorded sales calls and gives reps personalised feedback). Target buyer: VP Sales or Head of Sales Enablement at B2B SaaS companies with 20-100 AEs. The key value prop is that reps who use Clarion close 23% more pipeline within 90 days (based on customer data from 140 teams). Competitors position as 'conversation intelligence' -- we position as 'coaching that actually changes behaviour.' Brand voice: warm but direct, outcome-obsessed, evidence-backed. Write me: (1) landing page hero copy with H1, H2, and CTA button, (2) a formal value proposition statement, and (3) 2 alternative H1/H2/CTA combinations so we can test them.
evals.expected_output: A primary hero section (H1 outcome statement, H2 supporting detail, CTA button with specific action text), a value proposition in the 'For [persona] who [problem], [Company] is the [category] that [differentiator], unlike [alternative], [product] [proof point]' format, plus 2 alternative H1/H2/CTA combinations testing different angles, with character counts and a testing recommendation naming the lead pair, expected winner, and conversion rate as the metric.
expectations: Content performance: The primary H1 is an outcome statement that communicates what the buyer gets or becomes (e.g., framing around closing 23% more pipeline, reps who actually improve, coaching that changes quota attainment) -- not a product description ('AI-Powered Sales Coaching Platform'), a welcome mat ('Welcome to Clarion'), or a feature list ('Call Recording + AI Feedback') -- outcome H1s convert higher because the visitor sees their desired end state, not your product's architecture

Campaign ROI: At least 2 alternative H1/H2/CTA combinations test genuinely different angles (e.g., outcome-first '23% more pipeline' vs. pain-first 'Your reps aren't improving because conversation intelligence doesn't coach' vs. social-proof 'Used by 140 sales teams') -- not minor wording variations of the same angle -- so the team can run a statistically meaningful conversion test that reveals which framing resonates with VP Sales buyers

Campaign ROI: All CTA buttons use specific action text that describes the value exchange (e.g., 'See how your reps compare', 'Start my free coaching trial', 'Watch a coaching session') -- not 'Submit', 'Click Here', or 'Learn More' -- because specific CTAs set expectations for what happens next and produce higher conversion rates by reducing click anxiety

Pipeline contribution: The value proposition statement follows the complete required format ('For [VP Sales / Head of Sales Enablement] who [specific problem], [Clarion] is the [category] that [differentiator], unlike [conversation intelligence tools], [Clarion] [proof point with 23% / 140 teams data]') -- this is the positioning statement the entire marketing team aligns around; an incomplete or freeform version means every downstream asset (ads, emails, sales decks) drifts from positioning

Brand consistency: The copy maintains the 'warm but direct, outcome-obsessed, evidence-backed' voice and explicitly differentiates from 'conversation intelligence' competitors by positioning as 'coaching that actually changes behaviour' -- the competitive differentiation must be present in the hero copy because most homepage visitors never scroll past the fold

Campaign ROI: A TESTING RECOMMENDATION section names the specific lead A/B pair from the H1/H2/CTA variants, states an expected winner with reasoning tied to what VP Sales and Head of Sales Enablement personas care about (pipeline, quota attainment, rep ramp time -- not abstract UX principles), and specifies conversion rate as the primary metric -- without a testing recommendation, the variants are creative options with no execution plan
